name: German Grammar in Detail
tagline: Articles, pronouns, prepositions & nouns in depth

help.intro: Personal pronouns replace a noun and change with the grammatical case: nominative (the subject), accusative (the direct object) and dative (the indirect object). Learn each pronoun as a set across the cases.

tips.title: Which case?
tips.text: Nominative = who does it (Wer?), accusative = who/what is affected (Wen/Was?), dative = to/for whom (Wem?).

tips.title: Sound pattern
tips.text: ich → mich → mir, du → dich → dir: the accusative often ends in -ch and the dative in -r.
Ich sehe dich. (accusative)   Ich gebe dir das Buch. (dative)

endingPatternTables.title: Definite article endings (der/die/das)
notes: The final letters (-er, -ie, -as, -en, -em, -es) are the actual case/gender endings — the same endings reappear below on possessive pronouns.

endingPatternTables.title: Possessive pronoun endings (mein-/dein-/sein-/...)
notes: Add the ending to the possessive stem: mein + -em = meinem (masc./neut. dative).
Same endings as the der/die/das table above, except masc. nominative, neut. nominative, and neut. accusative take no ending at all (mein, not "meiner"/"meines").

help.intro: Every German noun has a gender — der (masculine), die (feminine), das (neuter) — and the article changes with the grammatical case. The table shows the definite article for each gender in every case.

tips.title: Accusative: only "der" changes
tips.text: In the accusative, masculine der → den. die, das and the plural stay the same as the nominative.

tips.title: Dative endings
tips.text: Dative: dem (m/n), der (f), and den + an extra -n on the plural noun (die Kinder → den Kindern).

tips.title: Color code
tips.text: Blue = der (m), red = die (f), green = das (n) — the same colors used across the app.

help.subtitle: Pronoun forms by case, how to detect the case (Wen?/Wem?), verbs bound to the dative and accusative, and pronoun word order — exportable as a PDF study sheet.
help.intro: This quiz mixes pronouns and articles: read the question and choose the correct accusative or dative form. The case depends on the verb and on what the question asks.

tips.title: Accusative vs. dative
tips.text: The direct object (Wen/Was? — whom/what) takes the accusative; the indirect object (Wem? — to/for whom) takes the dative.
Wen siehst du? — Ich sehe ihn. (acc)   Wem hilfst du? — Ich helfe ihm. (dat)

tips.title: Dative verbs
tips.text: A few common verbs always take the dative: helfen, danken, gefallen, gehören, antworten.

endingPatternTables.title: How to Find the Case
rows: Er sieht mich. → Wer sieht? → er
Er sieht mich. → Wen sieht er? → mich
Er hilft mir. → Wem hilft er? → mir
notes: Ask "Wen?" to find the accusative object (mich, dich, ihn, sie, es, uns, euch, sie, Sie).
Ask "Wem?" to find the dative object (mir, dir, ihm, ihr, uns, euch, ihnen, Ihnen).
The nominative is always the one performing the verb.

endingPatternTables.title: Verbs That Always Take the Dative
notes: These feel like direct objects in English, but in German the object is dative.
Always use a dative pronoun after them: mir, dir, ihm, ihr, uns, euch, ihnen, Ihnen.

endingPatternTables.title: Common Verbs With the Accusative
notes: Most German verbs take an accusative (direct) object.
Use mich, dich, ihn, sie, es, uns, euch, sie, Sie after them.

endingPatternTables.title: Two-Object Verbs (Dative + Accusative)
notes: The receiver (person) is dative; the thing given is accusative.
Default order with nouns: dative before accusative — "Ich gebe dem Kind den Ball."

endingPatternTables.title: Word Order of Pronouns
rows: Nominative subject comes before the object pronoun
Accusative pronoun comes before the dative pronoun
Front the object pronoun for emphasis
notes: With two pronouns the accusative comes first: "Ich gebe es ihm." (not "ihm es").
A pronoun always comes before a noun: "Ich gebe ihm das Buch."

help.subtitle: All 27 prepositions grouped by case, with English meanings and usage notes — exportable as a PDF study sheet.
help.intro: German prepositions force a specific case on the noun that follows, and the article then changes to match. Three groups to know: accusative (durch, für, gegen, ohne, um), dative (aus, bei, mit, nach, seit, von, zu), and two-way (in, an, auf … — accusative for movement, dative for location). The "Case" column below shows each preposition's case.

tips.title: Accusative prepositions
tips.text: durch, für, gegen, ohne, um (+ bis, entlang) always take the accusative.

tips.title: Dative prepositions
tips.text: aus, bei, mit, nach, seit, von, zu (+ gegenüber) always take the dative.

tips.title: Two-way prepositions
tips.text: in, an, auf, über, unter, vor, hinter, neben, zwischen take the accusative for movement (wohin?) and the dative for location (wo?).

endingPatternTables.title: Accusative Prepositions (immer Akkusativ)
rows: Always accusative. Used for movement through a space ("durch den Park") or the means/cause of something ("durch harte Arbeit").
Always accusative. Marks the recipient or purpose of something ("ein Geschenk für dich") or a planned duration ("für zwei Wochen").
Always accusative. Used for opposition or contact ("gegen die Wand") and approximate clock times ("gegen acht Uhr").
Always accusative, though the article is often dropped entirely ("ohne Geld").
Always accusative. Used for surrounding/encircling ("um den Tisch") and exact clock times ("um acht Uhr").
until; up to; as far as
Always accusative when followed by an article, but is often used alone before time/place names without one ("bis Montag", "bis Berlin").

endingPatternTables.title: Dative Prepositions (immer Dativ)
rows: Always dative. Used for origin ("aus Deutschland") and material ("aus Holz").
Always dative. Used for location near a person or place ("bei meiner Oma") and simultaneous activities ("beim Essen").
Always dative. Used for accompaniment ("mit Freunden") and the means by which something is done ("mit dem Bus").
Always dative. Used for time sequence ("nach der Arbeit") and direction toward places ("nach Hause", "nach Berlin").
since; for (a duration up to now)
Always dative. Marks an action continuing from a point in the past to the present ("seit zwei Jahren") — used with the present tense in German.
Always dative. Used for origin ("von Berlin"), possession ("das Auto von meinem Bruder"), and the agent in passive constructions ("von ihm geschrieben").
Always dative. Used for direction toward people or specific places ("zu meiner Freundin", "zur Schule") and occasions ("zum Geburtstag").

endingPatternTables.title: Genitive Prepositions (immer Genitiv)
rows: Takes the genitive in formal/written German ("während des Sommers"), though the dative is common in casual speech.
Takes the genitive in formal German ("wegen des Regens"), though the dative is very common colloquially ("wegen dem Regen").
Takes the genitive in formal German ("trotz des Wetters"), though the dative is common colloquially.
Takes the genitive ("statt eines Mantels"), though the dative also occurs informally. Often a shortened form of 'anstatt'.
Takes the genitive ("innerhalb eines Monats"), describing a time period or a bounded space.
notes: In casual spoken German, "wegen", "trotz", "während", and "statt" are often used with the dative instead of the genitive.

endingPatternTables.title: Two-Way Prepositions (Wechselpräpositionen)
rows: Two-way: accusative for movement onto a vertical surface ("an die Wand hängen"), dative for location at/on it ("an der Wand hängen"). Also used for days/dates with the dative ("am Montag").
Two-way: accusative for placing something onto a horizontal surface ("auf den Tisch legen"), dative for its location there ("auf dem Tisch liegen").
Two-way: accusative for moving to a position behind something ("hinter das Haus"), dative for being located behind it ("hinter dem Haus").
Two-way: accusative for movement into an enclosed space ("in das Kino gehen"), dative for being located inside it ("in dem Garten sein").
Two-way: accusative for moving to a position beside something ("neben den Schreibtisch stellen"), dative for being located beside it ("neben dem Sofa liegen").

Two-way: accusative for movement over/across something ("über die Brücke fahren"), dative for a stationary position above it ("über dem Tisch hängen"). Always accusative when meaning "about" a topic ("über das Wetter sprechen").
Two-way: accusative for moving to a position underneath ("unter das Bett laufen"), dative for being located underneath ("unter dem Bett liegen").

Two-way: accusative for moving in front of something ("vor das Haus stellen"), dative for a stationary position in front of it ("vor dem Haus stehen"). Always dative when expressing time ("vor zwei Jahren" = two years ago).
Two-way: accusative for moving to a position between two things ("zwischen die Bücher legen"), dative for being located between them ("zwischen den Büchern liegen").
notes: Wechselpräpositionen take the accusative when answering "wohin?" (movement/direction toward a place) and the dative when answering "wo?" (location/position).
"über" meaning "about" (a topic) is always accusative, regardless of movement.

subjects.display: through (movement through a place) / by means of
subjects.notes: Always accusative. Used for movement through a space ("durch den Park") or the means/cause of something ("durch harte Arbeit").
Always accusative. Marks the recipient or purpose of something ("ein Geschenk für dich") or a planned duration ("für zwei Wochen").
Always accusative. Used for opposition or contact ("gegen die Wand") and approximate clock times ("gegen acht Uhr").
Always accusative, though the article is often dropped entirely ("ohne Geld").

subjects.display: around (a place) / at (a specific clock time)
subjects.notes: Always accusative. Used for surrounding/encircling ("um den Tisch") and exact clock times ("um acht Uhr").

subjects.display: until (a point in time) / as far as (a place)
subjects.english: until; up to; as far as
subjects.notes: Always accusative when followed by an article, but is often used alone before time/place names without one ("bis Montag", "bis Berlin").
Always dative. Used for origin ("aus Deutschland") and material ("aus Holz").
Always dative. Used for location near a person or place ("bei meiner Oma") and simultaneous activities ("beim Essen").
Always dative. Used for accompaniment ("mit Freunden") and the means by which something is done ("mit dem Bus").

subjects.display: after (in time/sequence) / to (a place, esp. cities & countries)
subjects.notes: Always dative. Used for time sequence ("nach der Arbeit") and direction toward places ("nach Hause", "nach Berlin").

subjects.display: since/for (a point in time continuing to now)
subjects.english: since; for (a duration up to now)
subjects.notes: Always dative. Marks an action continuing from a point in the past to the present ("seit zwei Jahren") — used with the present tense in German.

subjects.display: from/of (origin, possession, or the agent in passive)
subjects.notes: Always dative. Used for origin ("von Berlin"), possession ("das Auto von meinem Bruder"), and the agent in passive constructions ("von ihm geschrieben").
Always dative. Used for direction toward people or specific places ("zu meiner Freundin", "zur Schule") and occasions ("zum Geburtstag").
Takes the genitive in formal/written German ("während des Sommers"), though the dative is common in casual speech.
Takes the genitive in formal German ("wegen des Regens"), though the dative is very common colloquially ("wegen dem Regen").
Takes the genitive in formal German ("trotz des Wetters"), though the dative is common colloquially.
Takes the genitive ("statt eines Mantels"), though the dative also occurs informally. Often a shortened form of 'anstatt'.

subjects.display: within / inside of (a time period or space)
subjects.notes: Takes the genitive ("innerhalb eines Monats"), describing a time period or a bounded space.

subjects.display: at/on (a vertical surface or edge) / to (a specific time)
subjects.notes: Two-way: accusative for movement onto a vertical surface ("an die Wand hängen"), dative for location at/on it ("an der Wand hängen"). Also used for days/dates with the dative ("am Montag").
Two-way: accusative for placing something onto a horizontal surface ("auf den Tisch legen"), dative for its location there ("auf dem Tisch liegen").
Two-way: accusative for moving to a position behind something ("hinter das Haus"), dative for being located behind it ("hinter dem Haus").
Two-way: accusative for movement into an enclosed space ("in das Kino gehen"), dative for being located inside it ("in dem Garten sein").
Two-way: accusative for moving to a position beside something ("neben den Schreibtisch stellen"), dative for being located beside it ("neben dem Sofa liegen").

Two-way: accusative for movement over/across something ("über die Brücke fahren"), dative for a stationary position above it ("über dem Tisch hängen"). Always accusative when meaning "about" a topic ("über das Wetter sprechen").
Two-way: accusative for moving to a position underneath ("unter das Bett laufen"), dative for being located underneath ("unter dem Bett liegen").

subjects.display: in front of (position) / ago / before (in time)
subjects.notes: Two-way: accusative for moving in front of something ("vor das Haus stellen"), dative for a stationary position in front of it ("vor dem Haus stehen"). Always dative when expressing time ("vor zwei Jahren" = two years ago).
Two-way: accusative for moving to a position between two things ("zwischen die Bücher legen"), dative for being located between them ("zwischen den Büchern liegen").

sentences.sentence: Wir gehen ____ den Wald, weil der Weg kürzer ist.
sentences.english: We walk through the forest because the path is shorter.

sentences.sentence: Er hat den Preis ____ harte Arbeit gewonnen.
sentences.english: He won the prize through hard work.

sentences.sentence: Der Einbrecher kletterte ____ das offene Fenster.
sentences.english: The burglar climbed in through the open window.

sentences.sentence: Sie hat die Prüfung ____ viel Üben bestanden.
sentences.english: She passed the exam through a lot of practice.
The gift is for my sister.

sentences.sentence: Wir bleiben ____ zwei Wochen in Spanien.
sentences.english: We're staying for two weeks in Spain.

sentences.sentence: Ich habe ein Buch ____ dich gekauft.
sentences.english: I bought a book for you.
He works for a big company.

sentences.sentence: Das Auto ist ____ die Wand gefahren.
sentences.english: The car drove into the wall.
We're meeting around eight o'clock.

sentences.sentence: Die Mannschaft spielt heute ____ den Tabellenführer.
sentences.english: The team is playing against the league leaders today.

sentences.sentence: Ich nehme eine Tablette ____ die Kopfschmerzen.
sentences.english: I take a pill for the headache.

sentences.sentence: Sie verlässt nie das Haus ____ ihren Schlüssel.
sentences.hint: Immer Akkusativ — Artikel ist hier vorhanden.
sentences.english: She never leaves the house without her key.

sentences.sentence: Er trinkt seinen Kaffee ____ Zucker, weil er Diabetes hat.
sentences.hint: Immer Akkusativ — hier ohne Artikel (unbestimmte Menge). Der Kontext (Diabetes) macht "ohne" eindeutig.
sentences.english: He drinks his coffee without sugar because he has diabetes.
I drink my tea without milk.

sentences.sentence: Sie ging ____ einen Mantel in den kalten Schnee.
sentences.english: She went out into the cold snow without a coat.
The children are sitting around the table.
The film starts at eight pm.
We were all sitting around the campfire.

sentences.sentence: Der Unterricht endet pünktlich ____ vierzehn Uhr.
sentences.english: The lesson ends punctually at two pm.

sentences.hint: Zeitpunkt als Grenze → "bis" (+ Akkusativ, oft ohne Artikel).
sentences.english: I'm on vacation until Friday.

sentences.sentence: Wir fahren mit dem Zug nur ____ Hamburg, nicht weiter.
sentences.english: We're only taking the train as far as Hamburg, no further.

sentences.sentence: Das Geschäft ist ____ zwanzig Uhr geöffnet.
sentences.english: The shop is open until eight pm.

sentences.sentence: Der Bus fährt nur ____ zur nächsten Haltestelle.
sentences.english: The bus only goes as far as the next stop.
My grandmother comes from Italy.
The table is made of wood.

sentences.sentence: Er nimmt das Geld ____ der Tasche.
sentences.english: He takes the money out of the bag.
The ring is made of pure gold.

sentences.sentence: Am Wochenende bin ich ____ meinen Eltern zu Besuch.
sentences.hint: Bei einer Person/Ort → "bei" + Dativ ("zu Besuch bei").
sentences.english: On the weekend I'm visiting at my parents' place.

sentences.sentence: ____ dem Frühstück lese ich gleichzeitig die Zeitung.
sentences.english: During breakfast I read the newspaper at the same time.
I was at my doctor's yesterday.

sentences.sentence: ____ der Arbeit höre ich gleichzeitig Musik.
sentences.english: While working I listen to music at the same time.

sentences.sentence: Ich fahre ____ dem Bus zur Arbeit.
sentences.english: I take the bus to work.
She goes for a walk with her dog.

sentences.sentence: Ich schreibe den Brief ____ einem Kugelschreiber.
sentences.english: I write the letter with a pen.
She likes playing with her friends.

sentences.sentence: ____ der Arbeit gehe ich ins Fitnessstudio.
sentences.english: After work I go to the gym.
We're flying to Berlin tomorrow.

sentences.sentence: ____ dem langen Konzert waren wir alle müde.
sentences.english: After the long concert we were all tired.
In summer we fly to Italy.
I've been learning German for three years.

sentences.sentence: ____ dem Unfall trägt er eine Brille.
sentences.hint: Zeitpunkt in der Vergangenheit bis heute → "seit" + Dativ.
sentences.english: Since the accident he wears glasses.

sentences.sentence: ____ einer Woche habe ich jeden Tag Kopfschmerzen.
sentences.english: For a week I've had a headache every day.

sentences.hint: Zeitpunkt in der Vergangenheit bis heute → "seit" + Dativ.
sentences.english: We've known each other since our school days.

sentences.sentence: Das ist ein Geschenk ____ meinem besten Freund.
sentences.english: That's a gift from my best friend.

sentences.sentence: Das Buch wurde ____ einem berühmten Autor geschrieben.
sentences.english: The book was written by a famous author.

sentences.sentence: Ich habe einen Brief ____ meiner Tante bekommen.
sentences.english: I received a letter from my aunt.

sentences.sentence: Das ist das Auto ____ meinem Vater.
sentences.english: That's my father's car.

sentences.sentence: Heute Abend gehen wir ____ unserer Tante.
sentences.english: Tonight we're going to our aunt's.

sentences.sentence: Was schenkst du ihm ____ seinem Geburtstag?
sentences.english: What are you giving him for his birthday?

sentences.sentence: Nach der Schule gehe ich ____ meinem Freund.
sentences.english: After school I go to my friend's.
For Christmas we visit our grandparents.

sentences.sentence: ____ des Sommers arbeite ich in einem Café.
sentences.english: During the summer I work in a café.

sentences.sentence: ____ der Pause sprechen wir über das Projekt.
sentences.english: During the break we talk about the project.

sentences.sentence: ____ der Vorlesung hat er die ganze Zeit geschlafen.
sentences.english: During the lecture he slept the whole time.

sentences.sentence: ____ des Films klingelte plötzlich mein Handy.
sentences.english: During the film my phone suddenly rang.

sentences.sentence: ____ des schlechten Wetters bleiben wir zu Hause.
sentences.english: Because of the bad weather we're staying home.

sentences.sentence: Sie kam ____ ihrer Krankheit nicht zur Schule.
sentences.english: She didn't come to school because of her illness.

sentences.sentence: ____ des Sturms fiel der Unterricht aus.
sentences.english: Because of the storm the lesson was cancelled.
He was criticised because of his mistake.
Despite the rain we're going for a walk.

sentences.sentence: ____ seiner Erkältung ging er zur Arbeit.
sentences.english: Despite his cold he went to work.

sentences.sentence: ____ des Lärms konnte ich gut schlafen.
sentences.english: Despite the noise I was able to sleep well.

sentences.sentence: ____ seines hohen Alters läuft er jeden Tag.
sentences.english: Despite his old age he runs every day.

sentences.sentence: ____ eines Mantels trug sie nur eine Jacke.
sentences.english: Instead of a coat she wore only a jacket.
He ordered tea instead of coffee.

sentences.sentence: ____ eines Autos kaufte er ein Fahrrad.
sentences.english: Instead of a car he bought a bicycle.
She drank water instead of a beer.
Within a month you must respond.

sentences.sentence: ____ der Stadt gibt es viele Parks.
sentences.english: Within the city there are many parks.
Within a week we have to move out.

sentences.sentence: ____ des Gebäudes ist das Rauchen verboten.
sentences.english: Smoking is forbidden within the building.

sentences.sentence: Er hängt das Bild ____ die Wand.
sentences.english: He hangs the picture on the wall.
The poster is hanging on the wall.
The sign stands by the street.
Hang the calendar on the door.
On Monday I have the day off.
Put the book on the table.
The book is lying on the table.
We'll see each other at the party.
Put the plates on the table.
The cat is sitting on the roof.
The dog runs behind the house.
The trash bins are behind the house.
Quickly hide behind the curtain!

sentences.sentence: Die Garage liegt ____ dem Haus, nicht davor.
sentences.english: The garage is behind the house, not in front of it.

sentences.sentence: Wir gehen heute Abend ____ das Kino.
sentences.english: We're going to the cinema tonight.
The children are playing in the garden.

sentences.sentence: Das Geschäft befindet sich ____ der Stadtmitte.
sentences.english: The shop is located in the city center.

sentences.sentence: Sie legt die Milch ____ den Kühlschrank.
sentences.english: She puts the milk into the fridge.
We live in a small town.
Put the chair next to the desk.
The dog is lying next to the sofa.
Come and sit down next to me!

sentences.sentence: Im Kino saß ich ____ meiner besten Freundin.
sentences.english: At the cinema I sat next to my best friend.

sentences.hint: Wohin?/Bewegung über etwas hinweg → Akkusativ: "über die Brücke".
sentences.english: We're driving over the bridge.
The lamp hangs above the dining table.

sentences.sentence: Wir haben lange ____ das Wetter gesprochen.
sentences.english: We talked about the weather for a long time.

sentences.sentence: Das Flugzeug fliegt hoch ____ die Wolken.
sentences.hint: Wohin?/Bewegung darüber hinweg → Akkusativ: "über die Wolken".
sentences.english: The plane flies high up over the clouds.

sentences.sentence: Wir lesen ein Buch ____ die deutsche Geschichte.
sentences.english: We are reading a book about German history.
The cat runs under the bed.
The shoes are lying under the bed.
The ball rolled under the car.

sentences.sentence: Wir saßen ____ einem großen Baum im Schatten.
sentences.english: We sat in the shade under a big tree.

sentences.sentence: Sie stellt das Fahrrad ____ das Haus.
sentences.english: She puts the bike in front of the house.
The bike is standing in front of the house.

sentences.hint: Zeitangabe "vor" + Dativ = "ago" (bei Zeit immer Dativ, keine Wahlmöglichkeit).
sentences.english: That was two years ago.

sentences.sentence: ____ einer Stunde hat es noch geregnet.
sentences.english: An hour ago it was still raining.

sentences.sentence: Wir treffen uns ____ dem Eingang des Kinos.
sentences.english: We are meeting in front of the cinema entrance.

sentences.sentence: Stell die Vase ____ die beiden Bücher.
sentences.english: Put the vase between the two books.

sentences.sentence: Das Auto parkt ____ den beiden Häusern.
sentences.english: The car is parked between the two houses.

sentences.sentence: Er setzte sich ____ die beiden Mädchen.
sentences.english: He sat down between the two girls.

sentences.sentence: Die Kirche steht ____ dem Rathaus und der Schule.
sentences.hint: Wo? → Ort/Lage (Dativ): "zwischen dem Rathaus und der Schule".
sentences.english: The church stands between the town hall and the school.
